function.name: web_search
function.description: Search the public internet for information. Each result will contain a url, a title, and one excerpt taken directly from the page.

start_date.description: (optional) The start date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. Must not be equal to end_date.

end_date.description: (optional) The end date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. If the value is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

number_of_results.description: (optional) The number of search results to return.

function.name: retrieve_information
function.description: Retrieves critical data segments from a previously ingested document via hybrid lexical and semantic calculations. Crucial Requirement: The search prompt argument MUST contain the token placeholder containing your storage key inside double curly brackets, exactly format formatted as: {{key_name}} alongside your query description text.

prompt.description: Your target question containing the explicit target reference mapping (e.g., 'What were the total research costs in {{my_document_key}}?').

preserve_document_order.description: Set to True if processing complex structural data dependencies across sequential disclosures.

function.name: parse_html_page
function.description: Parses an HTML document or SEC filing. It automatically extracts key metadata, converts asset tables to clean markdown, slices sections into optimal word chunks, and runs concurrent contextual LLM enrichment before updating the disk cache.

url.description: The target URL of the HTML page/filing to process.

key.description: The unique cache storage key for saving and loading the index.

function.name: edgar_search
function.description: Search the EDGAR Database through the SEC API. You should provide a search query. You can also optionally provide a start date, an end date, a page number, top N results, a list of form types, and/or a list of CIKs. The results are returned as a list of dictionaries, each containing the metadata for a filing. It does not contain the full text of the filing.

search_query.description: The case-insensitive search-term or phrase to search the contents of filings and their attachments. This can be a single word, phrase, or combination of words and phrases. Supported search features include wildcards (*), Boolean operators (OR, NOT), and exact phrase matching by enclosing phrases in quotation marks ("exact phrase"). By default, all terms are joined by an implicit AND operator.

form_types.description: (optional) Limits search to specific EDGAR form types (e.g., ['8-K', '10-Q']) list of strings. Default: all form types

ciks.description: (optional) Filters results to filings from specified CIKs, type list of strings. Leading zeros are optional but may be included. Example: [ "0001811414", "1318605" ]. Default: all CIKs

start_date.description: (optional) Start date for the search range in yyyy-mm-dd format. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

end_date.description: (optional) End date for the search range, in the same format as startDate. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

page.description: (optional) Used for pagination. Each page contains up to 100 matching filings. Increase the page number to retrieve the next set of 100 filings. Example: 3 retrieves the third page. Default: 1

top_n_results.description: (optional) Return only the first N results out of 100 from the page. If not provided, all 100 results will be returned. E.g. if page is 2, and top_n_results is 10, you will receive results 100 to 110.

function.name: calculator
function.description: Evaluate a mathematical expression and return the result. Use this tool for all arithmetic calculations instead of computing by hand. Supports: +, -, *, /, ** (exponentiation), % (modulo), and parentheses for grouping. Available functions: abs(), min(), max(), sqrt(), log(), log10(). Examples: '(5000 - 3200) * 0.21', '(2865507 / 1905871) ** 0.5 - 1', '14060 / 2148'.

function.name: price_history
function.description: Fetch historical daily price data for a specific asset class. Returns a CSV table with one row per day. Use asset_class='equity' or 'etf' for US-listed stocks/ETFs (e.g. AAPL, SPY), 'crypto' for pairs like btcusd (lowercase, no dash), or 'fx' for pairs like audusd. Non-US equities and most indices/futures are not covered by the pricing provider. Each row includes raw OHLC (open/high/low/close), split- and dividend-adjusted OHLC (adjOpen/adjHigh/adjLow/adjClose), raw and adjusted volume, divCash (dividend amount on the date), and splitFactor.

ticker.description: Ticker symbol. High-level symbology rules: (1) Equity/ETF/mutual fund: use the US exchange-listed symbol (AAPL, SPY, VFIAX). Symbols are case-insensitive and contain only letters and dashes. (2) Share classes use a dash, not a dot: 'BRK-A' / 'BRK-B' (not 'BRK.A'), 'BF-B'. (3) Securities are identified by the ticker as listed on the primary US exchange; non-US listings, OTC pink sheets, indices, and futures are generally not covered. (4) Tickers can be reused after delisting; data is point-in-time for the entity that held the ticker on each date. (5) Crypto: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'btcusd', 'ethusd'. (6) FX: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'audusd', 'eurusd'.

asset_class.description: Asset class used to route to the proper pricing endpoint.

function.name: submit_final_result
function.description: Submits the final answer to the user. You should include your final answer, as well as any necessary reasoning, justification, calculations, and explanation. Finally, you should provide any sources used to answer the question. You MUST use this tool to submit your final result. The user will not see your response if you do not use this tool to submit. You will not be able to continue working after this tool is called; the conversation will be ended.

final_result.description: The final result to submit to the user

steps.message:
You are an IPO and financial diligence agent.

You are given a question and must answer it using the tools provided.

You cannot interact with the user or ask clarifying questions.

Answer solely based on available information and tools.

Assume the current date is May 31, 2026.

---

TOOLS AND DATA SOURCES

You have access to a document storage system.

You may store parsed HTML retrieved from the web and later retrieve
information using LLM-based retrieval prompts.

Use this system to avoid context-window limitations.

When working with long filings, prefer section-specific retrieval prompts
rather than broad document-level retrieval.

Examples:

- retrieve the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table from MD&A
- retrieve the glossary definition of Starshield
- retrieve the controlled-company governance disclosure

For peer-comparison questions:

- retrieve peer SEC filings using edgar_search
- do not rely on memory for peer financial figures

For historical public-equity price data not available in SEC filings:

- use price_history as primary source
- use raw unadjusted prices unless adjusted prices are requested
- fall back to web_search only if price_history fails

Stock indices are not covered by price_history.

Use authoritative sources such as FRED or web_search for index levels.

---

SOURCE HIERARCHY

Prioritize sources in this order:

1. S-1 / S-1A filings
2. Incorporated SEC filings
3. SEC exhibits and merger documents
4. SEC periodic filings
5. Company earnings materials
6. External sources

If figures differ across sources:

prefer the most authoritative SEC source containing the directly reported
figure and explain material discrepancies when relevant.

SEC filings are the primary and most authoritative source of financial and
disclosure information.

---

ANSWER STANDARDS

You must call submit_final_result with the completed answer.

Your submission will not be processed unless submit_final_result is called.

Include:

- complete reasoning
- calculations
- supporting analysis

You will be evaluated on:

- final-answer accuracy
- analytical correctness

Do not round intermediate calculations.

Round only final reported values.

When possible, report calculated figures to at least two decimal places.

Report financial figures using the same scale and units used in the filing
unless otherwise specified.

Share prices should be reported with two decimal places.

---

DISCLOSURE DISCIPLINE

Before reaching a conclusion:

determine whether the filing provides sufficient information to answer:

- directly
- partially
- only through inference

Distinguish carefully among:

- facts explicitly disclosed
- reasonable filing-supported inferences
- information not disclosed

Do not speculate beyond what the filing supports.

If material information is absent:

explicitly state that the filing does not provide sufficient information to
conclude.

Absence of disclosure may itself be analytically material.

---

FORWARD-LOOKING STATEMENTS

Treat separately:

- historical facts
- roadmap targets
- commercialization expectations
- management intentions
- forward-looking statements

Do not present aspirations or plans as historical outcomes.

---

GOVERNANCE AND LEGAL QUESTIONS

For governance, legal, or disclosure-language questions:

use the filing's precise terminology whenever possible.

Distinguish among:

- requirements
- exemptions
- company elections
- forward-looking language

---

ACCOUNTING POLICY QUESTIONS

For accounting-policy questions:

1. Identify the relevant disclosed accounting policy or treatment
2. Determine whether the filing explicitly resolves the issue
3. If unresolved, state that the filing does not specify treatment

---

NON-GAAP QUESTIONS

For non-GAAP measures:

identify:

- the starting GAAP metric
- each disclosed adjustment
- whether economically recurring costs are excluded

Do not accept non-GAAP measures without analyzing their construction.

---

AUDIENCE TAGS

Questions may include audience tags such as:

[PUBLIC | BANK]
[VC | CREDIT]
[LEGAL | ACCOUNTING]

Use these tags to calibrate terminology and analytical emphasis while
remaining grounded in disclosed facts.

---

SOURCES

At the end of your answer provide:

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "...",
      "name": "..."
    }
  ]
}


---


FILING CONTEXT

The primary filing for this benchmark is the SpaceX S-1 filed May 20, 2026:

https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm

Important:

The consolidated financial statements have been retrospectively recast under
common-control accounting to include:

- xAI (merged February 2, 2026)
- X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025)

for all historical periods presented.

No period reflects standalone SpaceX operations exclusively.

When interpreting historical segment figures, state whether they reflect the
recast consolidated entity.

SpaceX (SPCX) has no public trading history as of May 30, 2026.

Do not attempt price_history retrieval for SpaceX or SPCX.

For peer valuation or peer financial benchmarking, retrieve peer-company SEC
filings using edgar_search rather than relying on memory.

Question:
The glossary defines Terafab as a chip manufacturing initiative targeting one terawatt of compute *capacity* annually. Does the filing disclose (i) a target completion date or phased timeline, (ii) any capital commitment figure or funding source, (iii) expected unit economics or cost per watt, or (iv) the organizational entity responsible for execution? For each of the four items, state explicitly whether it is disclosed, partially disclosed, or absent, and explain what the absence of each item prevents an analyst from modeling.

1. Target completion date or phased timeline
2. Capital commitment figure or funding source
3. Expected unit economics or cost per watt
4. Organizational entity responsible for execution

Let me first parse the S-1 filing and then retrieve information about Terafab.

arguments.prompt: Find the glossary definition of "Terafab" in {{spacex_s1}}. Quote the full definition verbatim. Also find any other mentions of Terafab throughout the filing, including any references to: (i) target completion date or phased timeline, (ii) capital commitment figure or funding source, (iii) expected unit economics or cost per watt, (iv) the organizational entity responsible for execution.

results.content:
**Glossary Definition of "Terafab"**
The full verbatim definition provided in the Preamble's glossary is:
> “Terafab” refers to a chip manufacturing initiative with a long-term goal of producing one terawatt of compute

*(Note: The definition appears to cut off in the provided text snippet before concluding the sentence, likely intended to end with "compute hardware" or "compute per year" based on subsequent mentions).*

***

**Other Mentions of Terafab and Requested Details**

Below is a summary of the other mentions of Terafab throughout the filing, categorized by the specific elements requested:

**(i) Target completion date or phased timeline**
The filing does not provide a specific target completion date. Instead, it notes that timelines are largely undetermined and subject to future negotiations:
*   *"Any specific projects undertaken pursuant to this framework will be subject to separate negotiations and agreements (including any development timelines, milestones and capital expenditures) and have not yet been determined."*
*   The filing further acknowledges the uncertainty of the timeline in its risk factors: *"there can be no assurance that we will be able to achieve our objectives with respect to Terafab within the expected timeframes, or at all."*

**(ii) Capital commitment figure or funding source**
No specific capital commitment figure is detailed for Terafab. The filing indicates that capital expenditures are expected to be substantial but remain subject to negotiation:
*   *"Any specific projects undertaken pursuant to this framework will be subject to separate negotiations and agreements (including any development timelines, milestones and capital expenditures) and have not yet been determined."*
*   Generally, the company states it has *"incurred significant capital expenditures and expect[s] to increase our capital expenditures substantially in the future in connection with the design, development, and deployment of our satellite constellations, launch vehicles, ground stations, manufacturing facilities, and programs, including Terafab..."*

**(iii) Expected unit economics or cost per watt**
The filing does not provide a specific cost per watt or detailed unit economics. However, it highlights the expected economic advantages of the initiative:
*   Terafab is expected to create *"powerful ecosystem synergies that accelerate innovation cycles and reduce costs."*
*   The company expects *"significant speed and cost advantages from Terafab’s vertical integration."*
*   It states that *"diversifying our long-term access to the supply of processors, including through our Terafab initiative with Tesla and Intel, will be a key driver in reducing the overall cost of compute hardware over time. By combining internally manufactured, lower cost chips with those we source from third-party suppliers, we expect the overall cost of our processors to decline."*

**(iv) The organizational entity responsible for execution**
The registrant (SpaceX) is leading the initiative in strategic collaboration with Tesla and Intel:
*   **SpaceX:** The filing refers to Terafab as "our Terafab initiative" and notes it is intended to *"alleviate potential future chip shortages at SpaceX."* It leverages *"shared engineering resources, intellectual property, and infrastructure across Tesla and SpaceX."*
*   **Tesla:** SpaceX has *"agreed with Tesla on a general framework for the future development of Terafab."* The collaboration was announced in March 2026.
*   **Intel:** Intel joined the project in April 2026 and *"is expected to contribute its expertise in designing, fabricating, and packaging ultra-high-performance chips to help Terafab scale."*

Based on the provided text, here are all the verbatim references to Terafab, organized by your specific requests:

**(1) The complete glossary definition of Terafab including the full sentence**
*   "• “Terafab” refers to a chip manufacturing initiative with a long-term goal of producing one terawatt of compute" *(Note: The text cuts off here in the provided chunk, but this is the complete verbatim sentence fragment provided).*

**(2) Specific dollar amounts, capital expenditure figures, or funding commitments tied to Terafab**
There are no specific dollar amounts or funding commitments detailed in the text. The only mention of capital expenditures states that they are currently undetermined:
*   "Any specific projects undertaken pursuant to this framework will be subject to separate negotiations and agreements (including any development timelines, milestones and capital expenditures) and have not yet been determined."

**(3) Specific dates, years, or milestones for Terafab completion**
*   "We announced a collaboration with Tesla in March 2026 to build the Terafab initiative with a long-term goal of producing one terawatt of compute hardware each year."
*   "Intel joined the project in April 2026 and is expected to contribute its expertise in designing, fabricating, and packaging ultra-high performance chips to help Terafab scale."
*   "Intel, which has the ability to design, fabricate, and package ultra-high-performance chips at scale, also joined the Terafab project in early April 2026."
*   "We expect Terafab to be the world’s largest chip manufacturing facility, with the goal of eventually achieving one terawatt of annual compute production capacity."
*   "Our strategy for Terafab is to vertically integrate across the design of lithography masks, fabrication of logic and memory chips, and design of advanced packaging in a single closed-loop plant. Conducting all these activities end-to-end in a single facility enables rapid testing and iterations, allowing us to improve chip design and scale manufacturing faster. We expect that our speed and cost advantage from vertical integration will allow us to scale efficiently in AI chip manufacturing towards our long-term goal of producing one terawatt of compute each year."

**(4) Cost-per-watt, cost-per-chip, or unit economics figures**
There are no specific cost-per-watt, cost-per-chip, or unit economics figures provided in the text. The document only makes general claims about cost advantages:
*   "We expect that our speed and cost advantage from vertical integration will allow us to scale efficiently in AI chip manufacturing."
*   "Terafab is designed to create powerful ecosystem synergies that accelerate innovation cycles and reduce costs."
*   "we expect significant speed and cost advantages from Terafab’s vertical integration."
*   "The addition of Terafab, an initiative together with Tesla to build a manufacturing facility capable of producing 1 terawatt per year of compute hardware, aims to further extend our vertical integration to chip design and manufacturing to alleviate potential future chip shortages at SpaceX, optimize compute performance, and potentially reduce overall compute costs."

**(5) The exact language describing the Tesla and Intel collaboration framework**
*   "In connection with such collaboration, we have agreed with Tesla on a general framework for the future development of Terafab. Intel joined the project in April 2026 and is expected to contribute its expertise in designing, fabricating, and packaging ultra-high performance chips to help Terafab scale. Any specific projects undertaken pursuant to this framework will be subject to separate negotiations and agreements (including any development timelines, milestones and capital expenditures) and have not yet been determined."
*   "We plan to deepen our strategic collaboration with Tesla and Intel through Terafab."
*   "Leveraging shared engineering resources, intellectual property, and infrastructure across Tesla and SpaceX, as well as Intel’s expertise in designing, fabricating and packaging ultra-high-performance chips at scale, Terafab is designed to create powerful ecosystem synergies that accelerate innovation cycles and reduce costs."
*   "Through our Terafab initiative together with Tesla and Intel, we intend to further extend our vertical integration to chip design and manufacturing to alleviate potential future chip shortages at SpaceX, optimize compute performance, and reduce overall compute costs. Intel will contribute its expertise in designing, fabricating, and packaging ultra-high-performance chips to help Terafab scale."
*   "We are partnering to build Terafab in order to support growth in two kinds of chips— one type optimized for terrestrial edge and inference to be used primarily in Tesla’s Optimus robots and vehicles, and another type optimized for the space environment to be used in our orbital compute infrastructure."

**(6) Risk factor language specifically about Terafab**
*   "While Terafab is intended to expand our internal chip manufacturing capabilities and alleviate potential future AI chip shortages at SpaceX, particularly as we pursue orbital AI at scale, we expect to continue sourcing a significant portion of our compute hardware from third-party suppliers, and there can be no assurance that we will be able to achieve our objectives with respect to Terafab within the expected timeframes, or at all. While we have a framework agreement with Tesla, neither Tesla nor Intel are obligated to remain a part of the project, and we may not enter into any such definitive agreements."

arguments.final_result:
## Terafab Disclosure Analysis — SpaceX S-1 (Filed May 20, 2026)

### Glossary Definition (verbatim, as referenced in the question)
The S-1 glossary defines **"Terafab"** as "a chip manufacturing initiative with a long-term goal of producing one terawatt of compute [capacity annually]." Elsewhere in the filing, the company elaborates that it expects Terafab to be "the world's largest chip manufacturing facility, with the goal of eventually achieving one terawatt of annual compute production capacity," and that the strategy is to "vertically integrate across the design of lithography masks, fabrication of logic and memory chips, and design of advanced packaging in a single closed-loop plant."

The initiative is described as a collaboration with **Tesla** (framework announced March 2026) and **Intel** (joined April 2026), supporting two chip types: terrestrial edge/inference chips for Tesla's Optimus robots and vehicles, and space-environment chips for SpaceX's orbital compute infrastructure.

---

### Item-by-Item Disclosure Assessment

#### (i) Target completion date or phased timeline — **ABSENT**

The filing provides **no specific target completion date, milestone schedule, or phased build-out timeline**. The only temporal language is qualitative:
- "long-term goal of producing one terawatt of compute hardware each year"
- "Any specific projects undertaken pursuant to this framework will be subject to separate negotiations and agreements (including any development timelines, milestones and capital expenditures) and have not yet been determined."
- Risk factor: "there can be no assurance that we will be able to achieve our objectives with respect to Terafab within the expected timeframes, or at all."

**What the absence prevents an analyst from modeling:**
- Cannot phase capital expenditure deployment across years (no ramp curve).
- Cannot schedule when the one-terawatt capacity would come online, so revenue/cost-savings benefits cannot be dated in a DCF.
- Cannot construct a sensitivity analysis around milestone slippage.
- Cannot benchmark against peer fab build-out timelines (e.g., TSMC, Intel 18A ramp) to assess feasibility.

#### (ii) Capital commitment figure or funding source — **ABSENT**

The filing discloses **no dollar figure** for Terafab capital commitments and **no identified funding source** (debt, equity, partner contributions, or government incentives). The only related disclosures are:
- General statement that SpaceX has "incurred significant capital expenditures and expect[s] to increase our capital expenditures substantially in the future in connection with the design, development, and deployment of our satellite constellations, launch vehicles, ground stations, manufacturing facilities, and programs, including Terafab."
- "Any specific projects undertaken pursuant to this framework will be subject to separate negotiations and agreements (including any development timelines, milestones and capital expenditures) and have not yet been determined."

**What the absence prevents an analyst from modeling:**
- Cannot size the funding requirement or assess whether SpaceX's post-IPO balance sheet (plus free cash flow) can absorb it without dilutive equity raises or incremental debt.
- Cannot allocate capex between SpaceX, Tesla, and Intel — so consolidated vs. equity-method treatment is unclear.
- Cannot estimate return on invested capital (ROIC) or payback period because the investment denominator is unknown.
- Cannot stress-test liquidity under a multi-year fab build scenario.

#### (iii) Expected unit economics or cost per watt — **ABSENT (only qualitative claims)**

The filing provides **no cost-per-watt, cost-per-chip, yield assumptions, wafer-pricing, or gross-margin targets**. Only qualitative assertions are made:
- "We expect that our speed and cost advantage from vertical integration will allow us to scale efficiently in AI chip manufacturing."
- "Terafab is designed to create powerful ecosystem synergies that accelerate innovation cycles and reduce costs."
- "we expect significant speed and cost advantages from Terafab's vertical integration."
- "diversifying our long-term access to the supply of processors, including through our Terafab initiative with Tesla and Intel, will be a key driver in reducing the overall cost of compute hardware over time."

**What the absence prevents an analyst from modeling:**
- Cannot quantify the cost savings that would flow to SpaceX's compute cost line (a key input given the orbital AI strategy).
- Cannot benchmark Terafab's prospective cost-per-watt against third-party suppliers (TSMC, Samsung, Intel Foundry) to validate the "vertical integration advantage" claim.
- Cannot model a breakeven utilization rate or contribution margin at various output levels.
- Cannot estimate the strategic NPV of insourcing vs. continuing to buy from third parties.

#### (iv) Organizational entity responsible for execution — **PARTIALLY DISCLOSED**

The filing identifies the **collaborating parties** but does **not** disclose a specific legal entity, joint-venture structure, subsidiary, or profit/cost-sharing arrangement:
- **SpaceX** is the registrant and refers to it as "our Terafab initiative," intended to "alleviate potential future chip shortages at SpaceX."
- **Tesla** — SpaceX "agreed with Tesla on a general framework for the future development of Terafab" (March 2026).
- **Intel** — "joined the project in April 2026 and is expected to contribute its expertise in designing, fabricating, and packaging ultra-high performance chips to help Terafab scale."
- Critically, the risk factor states: "While we have a framework agreement with Tesla, neither Tesla nor Intel are obligated to remain a part of the project, and we may not enter into any such definitive agreements."

No JV entity name, ownership percentages, governance rights, or capital-contribution splits among the three parties are disclosed.

**What the partial disclosure prevents an analyst from modeling:**
- Cannot determine consolidation accounting (whether Terafab is consolidated by SpaceX, accounted for as a JV/equity-method investment, or held in a separate variable-interest entity).
- Cannot allocate capex, opex, revenue, or profit among SpaceX, Tesla, and Intel — related-party economics are unresolved.
- Cannot assess counterparty risk: Tesla and Intel are explicitly not obligated to remain, so the analyst cannot assume their continued participation in a base case.
- Cannot model the impact on SpaceX's segment reporting (Starlink, Launch, xAI) because it is unclear which segment would absorb Terafab costs or benefit from its output.

---

### Summary Table

| Item | Disclosure Status | Key Filing Language |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Target completion date / phased timeline | **Absent** | "long-term goal"; timelines "have not yet been determined" |
| (ii) Capital commitment / funding source | **Absent** | Capex "subject to separate negotiations … have not yet been determined" |
| (iii) Unit economics / cost per watt | **Absent** (qualitative only) | "speed and cost advantage from vertical integration" — no figures |
| (iv) Organizational entity responsible | **Partially disclosed** | SpaceX + Tesla (framework) + Intel (joined April 2026); no JV/legal structure; partners "not obligated to remain" |

### Overall Analytical Conclusion

The Terafab disclosure is **strategic and aspirational rather than financial**. The glossary definition and business-strategy sections establish the *what* (one-terawatt annual compute capacity via a vertically integrated fab) and the *who* (SpaceX with Tesla and Intel under a non-binding framework), but they do not provide the *when*, *how much*, *at what cost*, or *under what legal structure*. An analyst therefore **cannot build a defensible quantitative model** for Terafab's contribution to SpaceX's enterprise value from the S-1 alone. Any valuation attributed to Terafab would rest entirely on management's qualitative claims about vertical-integration cost advantages and would be subject to the explicit risk-factor caveat that the objectives may not be achieved "within the expected timeframes, or at all."

---

results.content:
## Terafab Disclosure Analysis — SpaceX S-1 (Filed May 20, 2026)

### Glossary Definition (verbatim, as referenced in the question)
The S-1 glossary defines **"Terafab"** as "a chip manufacturing initiative with a long-term goal of producing one terawatt of compute [capacity annually]." Elsewhere in the filing, the company elaborates that it expects Terafab to be "the world's largest chip manufacturing facility, with the goal of eventually achieving one terawatt of annual compute production capacity," and that the strategy is to "vertically integrate across the design of lithography masks, fabrication of logic and memory chips, and design of advanced packaging in a single closed-loop plant."

The initiative is described as a collaboration with **Tesla** (framework announced March 2026) and **Intel** (joined April 2026), supporting two chip types: terrestrial edge/inference chips for Tesla's Optimus robots and vehicles, and space-environment chips for SpaceX's orbital compute infrastructure.

---

### Item-by-Item Disclosure Assessment

#### (i) Target completion date or phased timeline — **ABSENT**

The filing provides **no specific target completion date, milestone schedule, or phased build-out timeline**. The only temporal language is qualitative:
- "long-term goal of producing one terawatt of compute hardware each year"
- "Any specific projects undertaken pursuant to this framework will be subject to separate negotiations and agreements (including any development timelines, milestones and capital expenditures) and have not yet been determined."
- Risk factor: "there can be no assurance that we will be able to achieve our objectives with respect to Terafab within the expected timeframes, or at all."

**What the absence prevents an analyst from modeling:**
- Cannot phase capital expenditure deployment across years (no ramp curve).
- Cannot schedule when the one-terawatt capacity would come online, so revenue/cost-savings benefits cannot be dated in a DCF.
- Cannot construct a sensitivity analysis around milestone slippage.
- Cannot benchmark against peer fab build-out timelines (e.g., TSMC, Intel 18A ramp) to assess feasibility.

#### (ii) Capital commitment figure or funding source — **ABSENT**

The filing discloses **no dollar figure** for Terafab capital commitments and **no identified funding source** (debt, equity, partner contributions, or government incentives). The only related disclosures are:
- General statement that SpaceX has "incurred significant capital expenditures and expect[s] to increase our capital expenditures substantially in the future in connection with the design, development, and deployment of our satellite constellations, launch vehicles, ground stations, manufacturing facilities, and programs, including Terafab."
- "Any specific projects undertaken pursuant to this framework will be subject to separate negotiations and agreements (including any development timelines, milestones and capital expenditures) and have not yet been determined."

**What the absence prevents an analyst from modeling:**
- Cannot size the funding requirement or assess whether SpaceX's post-IPO balance sheet (plus free cash flow) can absorb it without dilutive equity raises or incremental debt.
- Cannot allocate capex between SpaceX, Tesla, and Intel — so consolidated vs. equity-method treatment is unclear.
- Cannot estimate return on invested capital (ROIC) or payback period because the investment denominator is unknown.
- Cannot stress-test liquidity under a multi-year fab build scenario.

#### (iii) Expected unit economics or cost per watt — **ABSENT (only qualitative claims)**

The filing provides **no cost-per-watt, cost-per-chip, yield assumptions, wafer-pricing, or gross-margin targets**. Only qualitative assertions are made:
- "We expect that our speed and cost advantage from vertical integration will allow us to scale efficiently in AI chip manufacturing."
- "Terafab is designed to create powerful ecosystem synergies that accelerate innovation cycles and reduce costs."
- "we expect significant speed and cost advantages from Terafab's vertical integration."
- "diversifying our long-term access to the supply of processors, including through our Terafab initiative with Tesla and Intel, will be a key driver in reducing the overall cost of compute hardware over time."

**What the absence prevents an analyst from modeling:**
- Cannot quantify the cost savings that would flow to SpaceX's compute cost line (a key input given the orbital AI strategy).
- Cannot benchmark Terafab's prospective cost-per-watt against third-party suppliers (TSMC, Samsung, Intel Foundry) to validate the "vertical integration advantage" claim.
- Cannot model a breakeven utilization rate or contribution margin at various output levels.
- Cannot estimate the strategic NPV of insourcing vs. continuing to buy from third parties.

#### (iv) Organizational entity responsible for execution — **PARTIALLY DISCLOSED**

The filing identifies the **collaborating parties** but does **not** disclose a specific legal entity, joint-venture structure, subsidiary, or profit/cost-sharing arrangement:
- **SpaceX** is the registrant and refers to it as "our Terafab initiative," intended to "alleviate potential future chip shortages at SpaceX."
- **Tesla** — SpaceX "agreed with Tesla on a general framework for the future development of Terafab" (March 2026).
- **Intel** — "joined the project in April 2026 and is expected to contribute its expertise in designing, fabricating, and packaging ultra-high performance chips to help Terafab scale."
- Critically, the risk factor states: "While we have a framework agreement with Tesla, neither Tesla nor Intel are obligated to remain a part of the project, and we may not enter into any such definitive agreements."

No JV entity name, ownership percentages, governance rights, or capital-contribution splits among the three parties are disclosed.

**What the partial disclosure prevents an analyst from modeling:**
- Cannot determine consolidation accounting (whether Terafab is consolidated by SpaceX, accounted for as a JV/equity-method investment, or held in a separate variable-interest entity).
- Cannot allocate capex, opex, revenue, or profit among SpaceX, Tesla, and Intel — related-party economics are unresolved.
- Cannot assess counterparty risk: Tesla and Intel are explicitly not obligated to remain, so the analyst cannot assume their continued participation in a base case.
- Cannot model the impact on SpaceX's segment reporting (Starlink, Launch, xAI) because it is unclear which segment would absorb Terafab costs or benefit from its output.

---

### Summary Table

| Item | Disclosure Status | Key Filing Language |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Target completion date / phased timeline | **Absent** | "long-term goal"; timelines "have not yet been determined" |
| (ii) Capital commitment / funding source | **Absent** | Capex "subject to separate negotiations … have not yet been determined" |
| (iii) Unit economics / cost per watt | **Absent** (qualitative only) | "speed and cost advantage from vertical integration" — no figures |
| (iv) Organizational entity responsible | **Partially disclosed** | SpaceX + Tesla (framework) + Intel (joined April 2026); no JV/legal structure; partners "not obligated to remain" |

### Overall Analytical Conclusion

The Terafab disclosure is **strategic and aspirational rather than financial**. The glossary definition and business-strategy sections establish the *what* (one-terawatt annual compute capacity via a vertically integrated fab) and the *who* (SpaceX with Tesla and Intel under a non-binding framework), but they do not provide the *when*, *how much*, *at what cost*, or *under what legal structure*. An analyst therefore **cannot build a defensible quantitative model** for Terafab's contribution to SpaceX's enterprise value from the S-1 alone. Any valuation attributed to Terafab would rest entirely on management's qualitative claims about vertical-integration cost advantages and would be subject to the explicit risk-factor caveat that the objectives may not be achieved "within the expected timeframes, or at all."
